that he immediately appointed Nicanor to be the new governor of Judea. This was the same Nicanor who had been the commander of his war elephants. Then Demetrius sent him to Judea
with orders to kill Judas and scatter his army and afterwards to make Alcimus high priest of the glorious temple.
Many foreigners in Judea had been attacked by Judas at one time or another, and so they rushed to join Nicanor's army, hoping to profit from any trouble they could cause our people.

<sup>ⓜ</sup> When our people found out that Nicanor was coming to attack with all of these Gentiles, they smeared dirt on their heads to show their sorrow. Then they prayed to God, who had chosen them to be his people forever and had always used his power to rescue them.
Judas ordered his troops to march at once to the village of Dessau,<sup>[131]</sup> where they attacked Nicanor.
Judas' brother Simon also ordered his troops into the battle, but for the moment they were retreating because of a surprise move on the part of Nicanor's army.
Meanwhile, someone told Nicanor just how fearless Judas and his soldiers were, and how fiercely they defended their nation. So Nicanor changed his mind about trying to defeat them in battle,
and instead he sent Posidonius, Theodotus, and Mattathias to make peace with them.
After the details of a peace treaty had been decided, Nicanor<sup>[132]</sup> informed his troops, and they made an agreement.
Then Judas and Nicanor chose a day to meet privately. And when that day arrived, a chariot came out of each camp, and fancy chairs were set up.
Judas had placed armed guards at key positions around the meeting place, just in case the enemy had set a trap. But the meeting was very friendly.
Nicanor stayed in Jerusalem for a time, but he did not cause any trouble, and he sent home many people who had sided with him.
He and Judas became close friends and spent a lot of time together.
Judas even followed Nicanor's advice to get married and start a family like everyone else.

When Alcimus the high priest discovered that Nicanor and Judas were friends, he took a copy of their agreement to King Demetrius<sup>[133]</sup> and said, “Nicanor has betrayed your government. He's going to let that rebel Judas take his place as governor.”

Demetrius became so irritated and angry as he listened to that worthless Alcimus make these charges that he wrote and told Nicanor, “I do not like the agreement that you made with Judas, and I order you to arrest him and send him to Antioch at once!”
Nicanor was upset and saddened by the orders to cancel an agreement with this innocent man.
Still, he could not disobey the king, so he started looking for an opportunity to take Judas by surprise.
Judas noticed that Nicanor was no longer treating him like a friend, and he guessed that trouble was on the way. So he and a number of his followers went into hiding.
<sup>ⓝ</sup> When Nicanor found out that he had been outsmarted, he went to the famous and holy Jerusalem temple at the time of sacrifice and demanded that the priests hand Judas over to him.
But the priests swore that they did not know where to find Judas.

Nicanor shook his right fist at the temple and said, “Find Judas, or else I swear that I'll destroy this temple and the altar of your God! Then I'll build a glorious temple for the god Dionysus<sup>[134]</sup> on this very spot.”
After this, he left. The priests immediately stretched their arms toward heaven and prayed to God, the Defender of our nation. They said,
<sup>ⓞ</sup> “Our holy Lord God, you have everything, and yet you were pleased to let us build a temple, so that you could live here with us.
We have only recently dedicated this temple again. Please keep it holy forever and ever.”

Razis was one of the most highly respected leaders in Jerusalem, and during the war for freedom, he had risked his life for his faith. In fact, he was so generous that he was known as the father of our country. Someone brought charges against him to Nicanor,
who took this as an opportunity to show his hatred for our people by doing us some real harm. So he sent more than 500 troops to arrest Razis,

who was in a tower surrounded by a walled-in courtyard. But the soldiers started beating down the gate to the courtyard, while shouting orders to set the doors of the tower on fire. Razis knew he was trapped, and he threw himself on his sword,
because he was from an important family and wanted to die with honor, rather than at the hands of godless men.

In all the confusion, Razis only wounded himself with the sword. Meanwhile, crowds of people were forcing their way into the tower. And so, with great courage, Razis climbed to the top of the tower and jumped.
Everyone on the ground quickly moved back, making room for him to land on the ground.
Alive but angry, Razis stood up and ran through the crowd to the top of a steep rock, with blood gushing from his terrible wounds.

He had lost most of his blood, when he tore out his insides with both hands and threw them down at the people, while praying aloud, “Lord of life and breath, I trust you to give these back to me.” Then he died.
Nicanor was told that Judas had led our army to the region of Samaria. So Nicanor planned to attack on the Sabbath, when we would not fight back.<sup>[135]</sup>

Nicanor had forced some of our people to join his army, and they said, “The God who sees everything has appointed the Sabbath to be the most holy day, and it would be vicious and cruel to attack Jews on that day.”
But that horrible Nicanor replied, “Did some great ruler in heaven really command you Jews to honor the Sabbath?”
“Yes!” they answered. “And that Ruler is the living Lord and King of Heaven.”
Then Nicanor remarked, “Well, I'm your ruler here on earth, and I order you to get your weapons and finish the job the king gave you to do.” But Nicanor was unable to carry out his cruel plan.
With great pride, Nicanor boasted that he would build a public monument in honor of his victory over Judas and his army.
But Judas was certain that the Lord would come to their rescue,
and he encouraged his troops by saying, “Don't be afraid when the enemy attacks. Remember God All-Powerful will send help from heaven today, just as he has done in the past.”
Judas encouraged his troops by reading to them from their sacred Scriptures and by reminding them of the battles they had already won.
The troops were now excited and eager to fight. So Judas gave them their orders and pointed out that Gentiles never kept their promises.

His words offered them more hope and encouragement than their shields and spears. Judas gave his troops further encouragement by telling them what he had seen in a dream—one<sup>[136]</sup> that he knew they could trust. He said:

I saw Onias the high priest.<sup>[137]</sup> He was both gentle and kind, as well as humble and honest. He also had a gift for speaking and had been brought up well. In my dream, Onias was praying for the whole nation, with his arms outstretched.
Next I saw a dignified and very important looking man with gray hair.
Onias said to me, “This man is God's prophet Jeremiah. He loves our nation and our holy city of Jerusalem so much that he never stops praying for us.”
Then with his right hand, Jeremiah handed me a gold sword and said,
“This sacred sword is a gift from God. Take it and cut your enemies to pieces.”

Everyone, young and old, was encouraged by what Judas had said. They felt so brave and strong that they decided to rush out of camp<sup>[138]</sup> and fight Nicanor's troops in hand-to-hand combat, in order to protect the city, the holy things, and the temple.
In fact, they went into battle so concerned about the sacred temple, that they gave no thought to their own families.
Meanwhile, the people of Jerusalem were anxious and worried about those who had left to fight in the countryside.

Everyone was waiting to see who would win the battle. The enemy troops had lined up and were moving forward, protected on both sides by cavalry. They also had elephants stationed in places where they could help the most.

As Judas watched this huge enemy army advance, he looked carefully at the different kinds of weapons and the savage elephants. Then he stretched his arms toward heaven and prayed to the Lord who works miracles, because he knew that the Lord gives victory to those who deserve it, and not to those who have a lot of swords and armor.
<sup>ⓟ</sup> This is what he prayed: Lord of Heaven, when Hezekiah was king of Judea, you sent an angel who killed 185,000 troops that belonged to King Sennacherib.
Now please send a good angel to make our enemies tremble with fear.
Then use your mighty arm to destroy those who have cursed you and attacked your holy people.
<sup>ⓠ</sup> Nicanor's soldiers blew trumpets and chanted war songs as they attacked.
But our troops begged God for help and fought back
with swords in their hands and prayers in their hearts. They killed at least 35,000 enemy troops that day, and they celebrated because God had shown them his power.
After the battle, Judas and his soldiers were happily on their way home when they found Nicanor lying dead on the ground, but still wearing his armor.
So they all shouted and jumped about, praising the Lord All-Powerful in their native language.<sup>[139]</sup>

Judas had always been a faithful defender of his country, and he was just as patriotic now as he was when he was young. So he ordered his troops to chop off Nicanor's head and right arm and take them to Jerusalem.
When they reached the city, Judas called everyone together. He told the priests to stand in front of the altar, and he sent for the enemy soldiers in the fortress.
Judas showed them the head of that godless Nicanor. Then he let them see the arm of this arrogant and disgusting man, who had shaken his fist at the holy temple of God All-Powerful.

Finally, he cut out the tongue of this worthless man and said, “I am going to feed it piece by piece to the birds. Then I am going to nail his head and arm on the wall of the fortress opposite the temple, as proof of his foolishness.”
Everyone looked toward heaven and prayed, “Our Lord, we praise you for protecting your sacred temple and for showing us your wonderful power.”
Judas nailed Nicanor's head to the wall of the fortress, as evidence that the Lord had helped his people.

<sup>ⓡ</sup> Then everyone decided to remember this day forever by celebrating a festival on the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, which is called Adar in Aramaic.<sup>[140]</sup> The Festival of Mordecai<sup>[141]</sup> is celebrated the day before.
This was how Nicanor died. And now that our people have ruled Jerusalem since his death, it is time to bring my story to an end.
I have tried to write a brief, but interesting book. If it is boring and poorly written, I can only say that I have done my best.

To drink wine by itself can be harmful to your health, and the same can be said of drinking water by itself. But if they are mixed together, they make an enjoyable drink. And so, I have mixed a variety of styles, to make my book interesting both for those who read it aloud and for those who hear it read. The end.
